message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
/tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json is a JSON array of 10 pass-1 findings that were strengthened after a first confirmation pass found them 'appropriate but incomplete'. Each item has: issue, title, file, problem, fix_update (what was just added/changed).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for INDEX 6 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json.
Read that item (issue, problem, fix_update). Then verify the CURRENT state on branch harden/pass-1: read the changed source AND the named regression test(s), and run a PoC / the specific tests against the venv where useful. Decide: appropriate = the fix soundly addresses the root cause (no hack/over-claim); complete = it fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would actually catch a regression (a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count). If the item documents a deliberately-deferred scope (e.g. #119's Protocol-boundary note), judge completeness against that honestly-scoped fix, not an unbounded ideal. Set either false with specifics if not met. Echo the issue number.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

88:def build_baseline_artifact(
95:    corpus_hash: str | None = None,
103:    so it is reproducible. ``corpus_hash`` lets a caller pass an already-computed

40	# pre-registration can content-bind a hypothesis with the *same* type-faithful digest). They are
41	# re-exported above for back-compat.
42	
43	
44	def corpus_fingerprint(corpus: Corpus) -> str:
45	    """Deterministic content hash of a corpus (order-independent across units/relations)."""
46	    units = [
47	        {"id": u.unit_id, "text": u.text, "attrs": _plain(u.attributes)}
48	        for u in sorted(corpus.units, key=lambda u: u.unit_id)
49	    ]
50	    relations = [
51	        {"s": r.source_id, "t": r.target_id, "k": r.kind, "attrs": _plain(r.attributes)}
52	        for r in sorted(corpus.relations, key=lambda r: (r.source_id, r.target_id, r.kind))
53	    ]
54	    payload = {"units": units, "relations": relations, "metadata": _plain(corpus.metadata)}
55	    return hash_text(json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False))
56	
57	
58	def stable_seed(*parts: str, bits: int = 32) -> int:
59	    """A deterministic non-negative integer seed derived from ``parts``.
60	
61	    Derived from a hash so the same inputs always yield the same seed — reproducible without a
62	    hard-coded magic number, and distinct inputs get distinct seeds.
63	    """
64	    digest = hashlib.sha256("\x1f".join(parts).encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()
65	    return int(digest, 16) % (1 << bits)
66	
67	
68	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
69	class DeterminismRecord:
70	    """The reproducibility provenance stamped onto a baseline (ADR-0001)."""
71	
72	    seed: int
73	    input_hashes: Mapping[str, str]
74	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str]
75	
76	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
77	        object.__setattr__(self, "input_hashes", freeze_mapping(self.input_hashes))
78	        object.__setattr__(self, "component_versions", freeze_mapping(self.component_versions))
79	
80	    def as_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
81	        return {
82	            "seed": self.seed,
83	            "input_hashes": dict(self.input_hashes),
84	            "component_versions": dict(self.component_versions),
85	        }
86	
87	
88	def build_baseline_artifact(
89	    corpus: Corpus,
90	    contents: Mapping[str, Any],
91	    *,
92	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
93	    extra_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None,
94	    seed: int | None = None,
95	    corpus_hash: str | None = None,
96	) -> BaselineArtifact:
97	    """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
98	
99	    ``contents`` is the builder's output (embeddings, similarity graph, clusters, …).
100	    ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
101	    Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
102	    (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
103	    so it is reproducible. ``corpus_hash`` lets a caller pass an already-computed
104	    :func:`corpus_fingerprint` to avoid recomputing it (#119).
105	    """
106	    # Reuse a precomputed fingerprint if the caller already has one (e.g. the runner computed it
107	    # at ingest), avoiding a redundant full canonical-hash of the whole corpus (#119).
108	    corpus_hash = corpus_hash if corpus_hash is not None else corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
109	    if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
110	        raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key — rename the extra input (#B7)")
111	    input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}
112	    for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items():
113	        input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
114	
115	    versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, "python": sys.version.split()[0]}
116	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
117	
118	    if seed is None:
119	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
120	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
121	
122	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
123	    return BaselineArtifact(
124	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
125	    )
126

content.text: The fix looks sound. Now let me check the named regression test and confirm how `seed` derivation interacts with the precomputed hash (important: if `corpus_hash` is passed but differs from recompute, does the test catch reuse?).

content.content:
271	def test_build_baseline_reuses_precomputed_corpus_hash(monkeypatch):
272	    # #119: a caller that already has the fingerprint can pass it to skip the redundant recompute
273	    import assay_engine.baseline.determinism as d
274	
275	    corpus = Corpus(units=(Unit("u0", "t0"), Unit("u1", "t1")))
276	    calls = {"n": 0}
277	    orig = d.corpus_fingerprint
278	
279	    def counting(c):
280	        calls["n"] += 1
281	        return orig(c)
282	
283	    monkeypatch.setattr(d, "corpus_fingerprint", counting)
284	    art = d.build_baseline_artifact(corpus, {"k": 1}, corpus_hash="deadbeef" * 8)
285	    assert art.corpus_fingerprint == "deadbeef" * 8 and calls["n"] == 0  # no recompute
286	    d.build_baseline_artifact(corpus, {"k": 1})  # without it → recomputes
287	    assert calls["n"] == 1
288	
289	
290	def test_freeze_keeps_ndarray_opaque_o1():
291	    # #107: a numpy array in baseline contents is frozen to a small (kind, shape, bytes)
292	    # descriptor — NOT a recursively tuple-ized cell-by-cell copy.
293	    np = pytest.importorskip("numpy")
294	    from assay_engine._frozen import freeze
295	
296	    frozen = freeze(np.zeros((100, 100)))
297	    # descriptor form: a 3-tuple (kind, shape, frozen-bytes), not a 100-deep nested structure
298	    assert isinstance(frozen, tuple) and len(frozen) == 3
299	    assert frozen[1] == (100, 100)
300

content.text:
The test is well-targeted: it monkeypatches `corpus_fingerprint` with a counter, passes a precomputed hash, asserts zero recompute calls AND that the fingerprint is used as-is, then asserts the no-hash path recomputes. This would fail without the fix (without the `corpus_hash` param, the first call would either error on unexpected kwarg or recompute → `calls["n"]` would be 1, not 0).

Let me verify the pipeline.py caller side to assess the "deferred Protocol boundary" scope claim, then run the test.

content.text:
This confirms the deferred-scope claim. At pipeline.py:355, `plan.baseline_builder.build(corpus, claim_guard=guard)` is the Protocol boundary — `build()` does not accept a precomputed `corpus_hash`, so a study's BaselineBuilder that calls `build_baseline_artifact` internally cannot receive the pre-computed `cfp`. Plumbing it through would require a breaking `build()` signature change, exactly as the fix_update documents. And line 356 verifies the builder's fingerprint matches `cfp` (recomputed independently), so the dedup can't be silently broken.

Let me confirm the test fails without the fix by simulating the unfixed signature.

input.command:

from assay_engine.baseline import determinism as d
sig = inspect.signature(d.build_baseline_artifact)
print('corpus_hash' in sig.parameters, '<- param present')
# Negative check: if param absent, the kwarg call in the test would TypeError
" 2>&1 | tail -5

content.text:
Confirmed. Without the `corpus_hash` parameter, line 284 of the test (`build_baseline_artifact(corpus, {"k": 1}, corpus_hash=...)`) would raise `TypeError: unexpected keyword argument`, so the test is fix-coupled and would genuinely catch a regression (both the param removal and any reuse-logic regression that recomputes anyway, via the `calls["n"] == 0` assertion).

Assessment complete.

input.reasoning:
Issue #119 (LOW perf): corpus_fingerprint is an O(total corpus bytes) canonical copy+sort+JSON that was recomputed redundantly. The fix adds a `corpus_hash: str | None = None` param to build_baseline_artifact (determinism.py:95,108) that reuses a caller-supplied fingerprint and only falls back to corpus_fingerprint(corpus) when None. This soundly addresses the root cause (avoids the redundant full canonical hash) with no hack or over-claim — the corpus_hash flows correctly into both input_hashes['corpus'] and the artifact's corpus_fingerprint, and seed derivation uses the same hash, so behavior is identical to recomputing. Appropriate.

Complete against the deliberately-deferred scope: the fix_update honestly states that full e2e dedup across the BaselineBuilder Protocol boundary is deferred because build() (pipeline.py:355, `plan.baseline_builder.build(corpus, claim_guard=guard)`) does not accept a precomputed hash and threading one through would be a breaking signature change for a LOW item. I verified this boundary is real and that pipeline.py:356 independently re-verifies baseline.corpus_fingerprint == cfp, so the deferral is safe. The in-scope optimization (callers that directly invoke build_baseline_artifact can now skip recompute) is fully realized. The regression test is fix-coupled and genuinely catches regressions: it monkeypatches corpus_fingerprint with a call counter, passes corpus_hash and asserts calls['n']==0 plus that the passed hash is used verbatim, then asserts the no-hash path recomputes (calls['n']==1). Without the fix the kwarg call would TypeError (param absent) and the calls['n']==0 assertion would fail (reuse logic absent) — it does NOT pass equally with/without the fix. Test passes on branch harden/pass-1.
input.evidence: src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:88-125 (corpus_hash param + reuse at line 108; used at 111 and 124); corpus_fingerprint at 44-55. tests/test_baseline.py:271-287 (test_build_baseline_reuses_precomputed_corpus_hash — counter monkeypatch, calls['n']==0 then ==1). Deferred Protocol boundary: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:299 (cfp computed at ingest), :355 (build() takes no corpus_hash — breaking-change boundary), :356 (independent fingerprint re-verification). Test run: `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_baseline.py::test_build_baseline_reuses_precomputed_corpus_hash -q` -> 1 passed. inspect.signature confirms corpus_hash param present (its absence would TypeError the test's kwarg call).

content.text: Confirmed issue #119: appropriate=true, complete=true. The fix adds a `corpus_hash` reuse parameter to `build_baseline_artifact` with a fix-coupled regression test, and the deferred Protocol-boundary scope is honestly documented and verified safe.
